ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an anchovy food product class (FOODON_03302050) but no specific anchovy salad class; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers anchovy as a fish commodity; no composite anchovy salad code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for anchovy salad; none assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of anchovy salad is approximately 150 g, consisting of a plate of mixed greens topped with several anchovy fillets, olive oil dressing, capers, and lemon juice.

nutrient_source.title: Salad with fish, USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy Reference; anchovy component cross-referenced with USDA FDC NDB 15001 (Anchovy, European, canned in oil, drained solids)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact anchovy salad composite entry exists in primary regional sources. Values estimated from USDA FDC SR Legacy anchovy (canned in oil, drained) combined with a standard mixed-greens salad base (USDA FDC #11959) and olive oil dressing, weighted at approximately 35% anchovy, 50% greens, 15% dressing by mass. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; mixed salad with protein analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for anchovy salad. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous mixed green salad with protein (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database). The dish is predominantly non-starchy vegetables and protein-rich anchovies with olive oil dressing, all of which have negligible glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 3.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.72, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salt-cured anchovies
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit anchovy salad intake or request low-sodium anchovy preparations, as a 150 g serving provides approximately 1020 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Anchovy salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 15 and low carbohydrate content of 3.2 g per 100 g, resulting in a negligible glycemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids offset by high sodium from anchovies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Anchovy salad provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and monounsaturated fats from olive oil, but the high sodium content from salt-cured anchovies warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who must restrict sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Anchovy salad is a Mediterranean-origin dish, particularly associated with Italian cuisine, consisting of salt-cured or oil-packed anchovy fillets arranged over a bed of mixed salad greens, typically dressed with extra-virgin olive oil, lemon juice or red wine vinegar, capers, garlic, and freshly ground black pepper. The dish requires no cooking and is assembled cold. Anchovies contribute an intensely savoury, umami-rich, and slightly salty flavour, while the greens provide a fresh, slightly bitter counterpoint. The texture contrasts soft, oily fish fillets with crisp raw vegetables. Nutritionally, anchovy salad is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. It is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and calcium. The olive oil dressing adds monounsaturated fats. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content, derived from the salt-curing process used to preserve anchovies, making it a dish that hypertensive individuals should consume in moderation. For individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is highly suitable due to its very low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load. In Singapore, this dish appears on Italian-style café menus such as The Light Cafe. The Burmese name ငါးနီတူသုပ် reflects a similar anchovy-based salad concept in Burmese cuisine.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in anchovy salad?
Is anchovy salad good for heart health?
What are the calories in anchovy salad?
Is anchovy salad high in protein?
What does anchovy salad taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact anchovy salad composite entry was found in primary regional sources (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FDC SR Legacy anchovy (canned in oil, drained) combined with a standard mixed-greens salad base and olive oil dressing. GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed green salad with protein. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish estimated from component data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FDC SR Legacy component data (anchovy canned in oil drained + mixed greens + olive oil dressing) at approximate mass fractions of 35% anchovy, 50% greens, 15% dressing. Direct composite measurement is not available in approved primary sources. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source database; anchovy does contain some vitamin D but the composite value is not calculable with sufficient precision from available data. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FDC SR Legacy data for the component ingredients — confirmed zero. 4. GI is estimated at 15 by analogy with mixed green salad with protein from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for anchovy salad exists in approved sources. 5. The input food_name contained a Burmese script annotation (ငါးနီတူသုပ်) suggesting a Burmese anchovy salad variant; this has been preserved in aliases. The canonical food_name has been set to the English form 'Anchovy Salad'. 6. The input cuisine tag 'Italian' has been retained as the primary cuisine classification, consistent with the restaurant context (The Light Cafe). 7. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore field survey source context, even though the dish origin is Italian. 8. Sodium content is notably high (~680 mg/100 g) due to salt-cured anchovies; this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
